description: Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster (March 11, 2011) caused a sharp global decline in nuclear energy support. Pre-disaster support ~50% dropped to ~30-35% in weeks. Germany accelerated nuclear phase-out. Tests exogenous shock and fear-driven opinion collapse.

key_events.description: March 11, 2011: Tohoku earthquake and tsunami trigger Fukushima Daiichi meltdowns. 24/7 media coverage begins.
Hydrogen explosions at reactors 1, 3, and 4 broadcast globally. INES Level 7 (maximum severity). Public fear peaks.
Germany announces moratorium on nuclear plants, begins accelerated phase-out (Energiewende). Anti-nuclear protests surge across Europe.
Japan shuts down all 54 nuclear reactors for safety reviews. Switzerland and Italy also abandon nuclear plans.
Partial stabilization of opinion as energy cost concerns emerge. Pro-nuclear voices cite climate benefits but recovery is slow.

calibration_notes: Tests catastrophic exogenous shock dynamics. Key patterns: sudden ~15pt opinion collapse in 1-2 weeks, availability heuristic dominating risk perception, geographic proximity effect (stronger in Europe/Asia than US), policy cascade across countries, slow partial recovery driven by energy security and climate arguments. Gallup, Pew, and Eurobarometer data used. Recovery took 5+ years to reach pre-Fukushima levels in some countries.
